questionText: NEC Table 450.3(A) provides overcurrent protection requirements for transformers rated over what voltage?
explanation: NEC Table 450.3(A) applies to transformers rated over 1000 volts. These are medium-voltage and high-voltage transformers commonly found in utility substations and large commercial/industrial facilities. Table 450.3(B) covers transformers rated 1000 volts or less.
sparkyTip: Two tables, one cutoff: 450.3(A) = over 1000V, 450.3(B) = 1000V or less. The (A) table has much higher protection percentages because medium-voltage transformers handle faults differently.

questionText: Per Table 450.3(A), what is the maximum primary fuse rating for a transformer with impedance not more than 6%, installed at any location?
explanation: Per Table 450.3(A), for a transformer with impedance not more than 6% at any location, the maximum primary fuse rating is 300% of the transformer-rated primary current. The 600% value is for circuit breakers, not fuses.
sparkyTip: Fuses blow faster than breakers trip, so fuses get a LOWER percentage (300%) compared to circuit breakers (600%). Faster device = lower setting needed.

questionText: Per Table 450.3(A), what is the maximum primary circuit breaker setting for a transformer with impedance not more than 6%, installed at any location?
explanation: Per Table 450.3(A), for a transformer with impedance not more than 6% at any location, the maximum primary circuit breaker setting is 600% of the transformer-rated primary current. Circuit breakers are permitted a higher setting than fuses (300%) because they have different trip characteristics.
sparkyTip: 600% for breakers, 300% for fuses — breakers get double! These high percentages exist because the protection is for transformer damage, not conductor protection.

questionText: In the context of NEC Table 450.3(A), what defines a "supervised location"?
options: A location with security cameras and alarms
A location where only qualified persons monitor and service the installation
Any indoor electrical room with a locked door
A location inspected annually by the AHJ
explanation: A supervised location, as referenced in Table 450.3(A), is one where conditions of maintenance and supervision ensure that only qualified persons monitor and service the transformer installation. This typically means utility substations, industrial plants, or institutional facilities with dedicated electrical staff.
sparkyTip: Supervised = qualified people watching over the equipment. This allows using primary-only protection at tighter settings because trained staff can respond to problems quickly.

questionText: Per Table 450.3(A), in a supervised location with primary-only protection, is secondary overcurrent protection required?
options: Yes — secondary protection is always required
Yes — but only for impedance over 6%
No — secondary protection is not required
No — but only if a circuit breaker is used on the primary
explanation: Per Table 450.3(A), in a supervised location with primary-only protection, secondary overcurrent protection is not required. This is the key advantage of the supervised primary-only option — it allows a simpler installation with tighter primary settings (CB at 300%, fuse at 250%) in exchange for eliminating the secondary device.
sparkyTip: Supervised location + primary-only protection = no secondary OCPD needed. The trade-off is tighter primary settings (300% CB / 250% fuse vs. 600% CB / 300% fuse).
